title: Brain-computer interface trials are taking off
gist: Brain-computer interface trials are scaling rapidly—implanted-volunteer counts more than doubled to ~150 since 2023, China became the first country to approve a BCI for medical use, and multiple companies (Neuralink, Synchron, Neuracle, Precision Neuroscience) plus academic groups are racing on speech-decoding and mobility applications, though durability and ALS efficacy remain unsolved.

insights.claim: The number of people with electrodes implanted in their brains has more than doubled since 2024 to an estimated ~150, up from a cumulative 67 volunteers across 21 research groups counted between 1998 and end-2023.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: A sharp acceleration in implant volume signals the field moving from one-off experiments toward scalable clinical pipelines—an early-stage growth inflection for BCI players.
insights.quote: My current estimation would be around 150 people,

insights.claim: China became the first country to approve a brain-computer interface for medical use, with Shanghai-based Neuracle obtaining approval for use outside clinical trials after trialing its BCI since November 2024.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: First-mover regulatory approval positions China and Neuracle ahead on commercialization—watch as a geopolitical and competitive marker in neurotech.
insights.quote: This year, China became the first country to approve a BCI for medical use.
insights.locator: Over the past couple of years

insights.claim: Neuralink announced it implanted its device in 21 people over the past two years, while Synchron is in trials across North America and Australia and Precision Neuroscience trials a surface-of-brain device.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Multiple well-funded private entrants plus academic groups indicate a maturing, competitive field rather than a single-player monopoly.

insights.claim: BCIs span a trade-off spectrum: more invasive implants give better signal but higher complication risk, ranging from implanted electrodes to surface electrodes to non-invasive electrode caps.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: The invasiveness-vs-signal trade-off frames how different companies' approaches will compete on safety, regulatory path, and addressable patient pool.
insights.quote: the closer you get to the neurons you want to record from, the better your signal will be.

insights.claim: BrainGate's two-decade trial has pivoted from 'point-and-click' cursor control to decoding speech, with Casey Harrell's device producing his cloned voice from prior recordings after nearly three years of use.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: The shift to speech decoding plus voice cloning marks a capability leap that expands BCI utility beyond basic cursor control.

insights.claim: Most BCIs to date have been implanted in people with spinal cord injuries rather than ALS, and in some ALS cases the devices eventually stopped working for reasons scientists do not understand.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Unexplained device failure in ALS is a key unsolved risk that constrains the addressable market and could gate regulatory expansion.
insights.quote: In some cases where the devices initially helped people with ALS—even someone who was completely locked in—the BCIs eventually stopped working. And scientists don't really know why.
